Why on earth would you kill another woman and destroy your own life over a man?

A 70-year-old woman has been charged over the killing of British expat Karen Carter, who died in her French home more than a year ago. Carter, 65, was attacked at the property in the village of Trémolat in Dordogne on April 29 last year. She had been on the verge of divorcing her South Africa–based husband, Alan Carter, now 66, while at the same time seeing Jean–François Guerrier, a 75–year–old retired business executive who lived close to her French home. The investigation into her killing was mired in mystery, but local media reported that a woman has now been formally charged with Carter's murder. The public prosecutor of Périgueux, Jacques-Edouard Andrault, told Sud Ouest: 'On July 2nd, the investigating judge in Périgueux formally charged a woman with murder.
  • 438 comments

The childhood sweethearts had been married for just four weeks when 28-year-old PC Harper was dragged to his death as three teens tried to steal a quad bike in 2019. The driver of the vehicle which caused PC Harper's death, Henry Long, received a 16-year sentence, and will not be eligible for the early release scheme.

Good outcome People can’t please themselves

Building boss Robert McCarthy, 59, and wife Amanda, 61, had lived in their quiet corner of Nazeing, Essex, for years before a row erupted that would culminate in their neighbours taking the handheld machine to the fence and hacking down dozens of Leylandii trees. The couple have now told the Daily Mail of their years of 'hell' after their rear-neighbours 'decided they actually had a secret garden - it was our garden'. When the Mail visited the picturesque village amid scorching temperatures last week, there was little outward sign anything was amiss. But upon stepping out into the McCarthys' otherwise well-kempt garden, the missing fence panels, ragged boundary covered in weeds and the final lonesome, lopsided-looking tree told its own tale.

Glad this lot find it funny because I know the victims and the rest of the British public don’t find it one bit amusing

Andy Burnham was today forced to rebuke his Cabinet ministers for joking about Labour letting prisoners out - as anger rages at the possible early release of PC Andrew Harper's killers. The new Prime Minister said it was 'unacceptable' for Wes Streeting and Shabana Mahmood to quip about Justice Secretary Alex Norris wanting to 'let them all out'. It came after Mr Streeting, the newly appointed Defence Secretary, said he was 'sorry for any offence inadvertently caused' for his remarks to Mr Norris. Ms Mahmood also said there was 'no ill intent' about the 'ribbing' of Mr Norris. But Tory leader Kemi Badenoch pointed to the overheard remarks as she led outrage at Jessie Cole and Albert Bowers being eligible for release halfway through their prison sentences. Both men were handed 13 years in custody for manslaughter after a trial into PC Harper's death at the Old Bailey in 2020. The Ministry of Justice (MoJ) has confirmed Cole and Bowers might be eligible to be released from prison halfway through their sentences.

A girl has told how she watched her friends die in front of her as they lay trapped upside-down in a car in a water-filled ditch before her father used Live 360 to rescue her. Adam Dudzinski, 17, and his girlfriend Chloe-Ann Naylor, 16, drowned after he lost control of a Ford Fiesta in Iwade, near Sittingbourne, Kent at around 8.30pm on January 3. But another girl, who cannot be named for legal reasons, survived after being found by her father who was using the Live 360 mobile app, which allows people to track each other's locations, Kent and Medway Coroner's Court heard.

A cut-price cruise missile containing engines bought from landfill is threatening to level the playing field in Ukraine's war against Putin's forces. FP-5 Flamingo missiles are allowing Zelensky's men to hit targets deep into Russian territory for the first time, as foreign correspondent Chris Pleasance's explains in the latest episode of the Daily Mail's War On Tape.   • Fabulous to see these beautiful earrings being worn again after 3000 years. This is what jewellery is for - to be displayed so we can marvel at the craftsmanship of those who made these wonderful items so those centuries ago. Claims of 'cultural appropriation' are nonsense - lots of jewellery is made from stones and metals that come from all around the world. Zendaya was not making any cultural link between the earrings and herself or the film. Who knows, Helen of Troy may have worn exactly the same jewellery? Enjoy this, appreciate it, let the jewellery live again instead of being locked in a glass cabinet in a museum.

  • "The Odyssey has already earned back its $250 million budget in its first five days in theaters with a global total of $282 million." STOP DOING THIS!!! NO - IT HAS NOT! The box office take is 50/50 with the cinema chains, so for the studio it's made $141m thus far. That's NOT $250m Moreover, the MARKETING is rumoured $125m over and ABOVE the budget. Thus the studio has SPENT $375m The movie needs to make $750m (that's $375m for the studio AND $375m to the cinema chains) to BREAK EVEN Meaning it's currently $470m SHORT of making ANY profit. You'd think after all this time the Mail would have figured out how the movie industry works but nooooooo, always with this idiotic misunderstanding.

Water in the evening, rather then the morning. Then the water can be absorbed overnight, with less evaporation. Lawns don't need watering, they turn brown but will recover when it rains again. Always cut your lawns on a high cut, to give them more resilience. Too many people scalp their lawns.

Shackleton should be more widely known.an absolute hero of the highest order.

A daring expedition to the bottom of the ocean has revealed the shipwrecks of Britain's greatest explorers as they have never been seen before. Researchers descended more than 1,000 feet (305 metres) beneath the Labrador Sea to find the remains of Quest, the ship where Ernest Shackleton died in 1922. From the safety of their submersible craft, scientists from the Royal Canadian Geographic Society (RCGS) became the first people in over 60 years to lay eyes on the ship. Then, less than a week later, the crew set out once again to find the remains of the Terra Nova, the ship that carried Robert Falcon Scott on his final expedition to the Antarctic .
  • 27 comments
  • 1 video
  • share Scans reveal the shipwrecks of Shackleton and Scott like NEVER before

Six beluga whales have been rescued from an abandoned theme park in Canada after officials threatened to euthanise them.  Marineland, in Niagara Falls, Ontario, Canada, has been closed to the public for two years, and in 2025 officials threatened to end the lives of 30 beluga whales who remained in the park.  But after years of campaigning, a mission to move them to new homes in the US kick-started on Monday, with six of the remaining beluga whales moved to facilities in the US, including the Shedd Aquarium in Chicago and Sea World in San Antonio.
  • 65 comments
  • share Six belugas rescued from abandoned Canadian Marineland
